coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots and Ag nanoparticles decorated g-C3N4 nanosheets for enhanced organic pollutants degradation under sunlight irradiation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Dadigala, Ramakrishna
Bandi, RajKumar
Gangapuram, Bhagavanth Reddy
Guttena, Veerabhadram
coredata.dc:description: In this work, novel ternary composites were prepared by depositing both carbon dots (CDs) and Ag nanoparticles (AgNPs) onto the surface of g-C3N4 nanosheets (CNNs). The as-prepared samples were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS), and photoluminescence (PL) techniques. The characterization results demonstrated that the CDs and AgNPs were well distributed on the surface of CNNs. The photocatalytic activities of all the samples were evaluated by degrading methyl orange (MO) and p-nitrophenol (PNP) in aqueous media under sunlight irradiation. Among the prepared samples, CNNs decorated with 2wt% CDs and 1wt% AgNPs showed higher degradation performance. The enhanced performance was attributed to the improved light harvesting capability and effective charge carrier separation at the AgNPs/CDs/CNNs interface. Superoxide radical species and photogenerated holes were found to be predominant reactive species responsible for the photodegradation of these pollutants. A suitable mechanism was proposed and discussed in detail.
coredata.dcterms:subject: AgNPs/CDs/CNNs photocatalyst
Sunlight photocatalytic activity
Effective charge separation
Photostability

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of BCN, CNNs, 1CCNNs, 2CCNNs, 4CCNNs, 0.5A2CCNNs, 1A2CCNNs, and 2A2CCNNs (a) and close up view of BCN and CNNs (b).
FTIR spectra of BCN, CNNs, 1CCNNs, 2CCNNs, 4CCNNs, 0.5A2CCNNs, 1A2CCNNs, and 2A2CCNNs.

XPS survey spectra of CNNs and 1A2CCNNs composite (a); high-resolution spectra of C 1s (b and c) and N 1s (d and f) for CNNs and 1A2CCNNs composite; high-resolution spectra of Ag 3d for 1A2CCNNs composite.
FE-SEM images of BCN (a), CNNs (b), 1A2CCNNs (c), and TEM images of CNNs (d), 1A2CCNNs (e), and high-resolution TEM image of 1A2CCNNs (f).
Elemental mapping images of 1Ag/2CD/CNNS; carbon (a), nitrogen (b) AgNPs (c) and oxygen (d) and EDS profile of 1Ag/2CD/CNNS (e).
UV–vis DRS of BCN, CNNs, 1CCNNs, 2CCNNs, 4CCNNs, 0.5A2CCNNs, 1A2CCNNs, and 2A2CCNNs (a); magnified image of UV–vis DRS (b).
PL spectra of BCN, CNNs, 1CCNNs, 2CCNNs, 4CCNNs, 0.5A2CCNNs, 1A2CCNNs, and 2A2CCNNs (a) and Time resolved transient PL spectra of CNNs, 2CCNNs, and 1A2CCNNs (b).

Adsorption property and photocatalytic activity of BCN, CNNs, 1CCNNs, 2CCNNs, 4CCNNs, 0.5A2CCNNs, 1A2CCNNs, and 2A2CCNNs toward the degradation of MO (a), and adsorption property and photocatalytic activity of PNP over CNNs, 2CCNNs, and 1A2CCNNs (b).
Kinetics of the MO (a) and PNP (b) degradation over different photocatalysts.
Recyclability experiments of 1A2CCNNs photocatalyst for MO degradation under sunlight irradiation.
Trapping experiments of active species for MO photodegradation in the presence of 1A2CCNNs under sunlight irradiation.
Schematic illustration for the possible photocatalytic mechanism of 1A2CCNNs under sunlight irradiation.
The kinetics of emission decay parameters of CNNs, 2CCNNs, and 1A2CCNNs.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots and Ag nanoparticles decorated g-C3N4 nanosheets for enhanced organic pollutants degradation under sunlight irradiation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Osmania University, Hyderabad 500007, Telangana state, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, novel ternary composites were prepared by depositing both carbon dots (CDs) and Ag nanoparticles (AgNPs) onto the surface of g-C3N4 nanosheets (CNNs). The as-prepared samples were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS), and photoluminescence (PL) techniques. The characterization results demonstrated that the CDs and AgNPs were well distributed on the surface of CNNs. The photocatalytic activities of all the samples were evaluated by degrading methyl orange (MO) and p-nitrophenol (PNP) in aqueous media under sunlight irradiation. Among the prepared samples, CNNs decorated with 2wt% CDs and 1wt% AgNPs showed higher degradation performance. The enhanced performance was attributed to the improved light harvesting capability and effective charge carrier separation at the AgNPs/CDs/CNNs interface. Superoxide radical species and photogenerated holes were found to be predominant reactive species responsible for the photodegradation of these pollutants. A suitable mechanism was proposed and discussed in detail.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor based photocatalysis is an attractive and environmentally benign method for environmental remediation and hydrogen evolution [1–3]. The visible light driven photocatalysts have received great attention in this regard [4]. As a new promising visible light active, layered material, graphite-like carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has recently drawn much attention due to its remarkable properties like excellent thermal and chemical stability, low cost, can be easily synthesized from abundant precursors and it found potential applications in water splitting and organic pollutant degradation [5–9]. However, it is regrettable that the high recombination rate of photogenerated charge carriers significantly limits the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4 and makes it non-viable for commercial applications [10,11]. To reduce the recombination rate and improve photocatalytic performance, several efforts such as enlarging the surface area by exfoliation, doping with metals, noble metals and non-metals, modification with carbon nanomaterials and coupling with other semiconductors were made recently [5–7,12–20]. Nevertheless, improving the visible light photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4 based photocatalysts to use them practically with high efficiency is still remains a great challenge.

Carbon dots, as a new class of carbon nanomaterials, gained the attention of researchers with their notable properties like low-toxicity, easy functionalizability, tunable photoluminescence, high biocompatibility and chemical inertness, and have been successfully utilized in bioimaging, sensing, light emitting devices and electrocatalysis [21–24]. Very recently, they have attracted much attention in photocatalytic applications [25] and several photocatalytic systems were designed by combining CDs with other semiconductors such as TiO2 [26], ZnO [27], BiVO4 [28], Bi2MoO6 [29], BiOCl [30], BiOBr [31] and BiOI [32] to enhance the photocatalytic activity of the semiconductors. The enhanced photocatalytic performances of these composites could be due to the upconversion of CDs, which enables the utilization of the full spectrum of sunlight, and the conjugated π structure of CDs, which facilitates the adsorption of organic dyes and exhibits an excellent electron accepting and transport properties [26,33]. In view of these properties, CDs can be considered as good co-catalysts for designing an efficient photocatalyst which promotes the wide spectrum absorption and reduces the recombination rate.

From the previous reports, coupling noble metal nanoparticles (Au, Ag, Pt) with different semiconductors is also found to be a good strategy to improve the photocatalytic performance, since they offer separation of photogenerated charge carriers and exhibit strong absorption in the visible region because of their surface plasmon resonance (SPR) [34–36]. Among them, AgNPs are extremely attractive because of their remarkable catalytic activity, as well as their cost-effectiveness [35]. Based on the above descriptions, to improve the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4, designing a ternary composite by depositing both CDs and AgNPs on to CNNs is expected to be a good strategy as it combines the attractive photocatalytic enhancing properties of both CDs and AgNPs.

In addition, recent studies reveal that the single and few-layered CNNs exhibited significantly improved photocatalytic performance over bulk g-C3N4 (BCN), due to larger surface area and high charge carrier mobility [37]. However, few reports have focused on this process so far. Shubin yang et al. fabricated CNNs by liquid exfoliation method from BCN for hydrogen production [12]. Xuping sun et al. synthesized CNNs by ultrasonication assisted liquid exfoliation of BCN toward the degradation of MO dye [38]. Here, we prepared two-dimensional CNNs using an ultrasonication assisted liquid exfoliation method and used as an excellent substrate for the preparation of binary and ternary composites.

The aim of this work is to synthesize a g-C3N4 based novel ternary composites with improved photodegradation efficiency by depositing both CDs and AgNPs on it. Firstly, we prepared a series of binary composites (CDs/CNNs) by depositing different amounts of CDs on CNNs surface by an impregnation-hydrothermal method. Then, the composite with higher photocatalytic activity (2wt% CDs/CNNs) was chosen for the preparation of ternary composites (AgNPs/CDs/CNNs) with different amount of AgNO3 by the photochemical reduction method. Structural, optical, and morphological properties of the prepared photocatalysts were investigated using various spectroscopic and microscopic techniques. The photocatalytic activities of synthesized catalysts were evaluated by degradation of MO and PNP under sunlight irradiation we found that the ternary composites were exhibited an improved activity over the binary and individual catalysts, highlighting the important role of AgNPs in enhancing the photocatalytic activity. The resulting 1AgNPs/2CDs/CNNs ternary composite displayed excellent photocatalytic activity, good stability and reusability even after four successive runs. The enhanced photocatalytic activity of the ternary composites can be ascribed to the excellent electronic transmission capacity of CDs and SPR of AgNPs. To further elucidate the photocatalytic degradation mechanism, a series of scavengers of the reactive species were employed. To the best of our knowledge, the preparation of AgNPs/CDs/CNNs for degradation of organic pollutants under sunlight irradiation has not been reported.

All the chemicals used in this study were of analytical grade and were used without further treatment and deionized water was used throughout this study. BCN was prepared by thermal polymerization of melamine at high temperature in a muffle furnace [38,39]. In detail, melamine was placed in a crucible and then heated at 600°C for 2h with a ramp rate of about 3°C/min. the obtained yellow agglomerates were collected and ground into fine powder. CNNs were prepared by liquid exfoliation of as-prepared BCN powder in water. In detail, 100mg of BCN powder was dispersed in 100mL of water, and the mixture was sonicated for 16h and then the resulting suspension was centrifuged at 5000rpm to remove the residual un-exfoliated g-C3N4. Thus the light yellow exfoliated CNNs were obtained.

CDs were synthesized by a simple autoclave using onion waste as a precursor according to our previous report [40]. The CDs/CNNs composites were prepared by an impregnation-hydrothermal method. In a typical synthesis, 100mg CNNs was dispersed in 30mL deionized water, which contains a certain amount of CDs and vigorously stirred for 4h at room temperature to obtain a homogeneous suspension. After that, the suspension was transferred into a 50mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 100°C for 2h and cool down to room temperature naturally. The obtained products were collected by centrifugation and washed with deionized water for several times, and dried at 60°C overnight. Following the same procedure, the CDs/CNNs composites with various amounts of CDs (1, 2, 4wt%) were prepared and denoted as 1CCNNs, 2CCNNs, and 4CCNNs, respectively.

The AgNPs loading on the 2CCNNs photocatalyst was prepared by a photochemical reduction method. In detail, 100mg of 2CCNNs were added to 30mL deionized water, which contains an appropriate amount of AgNO3. The suspension was subsequently stirred in dark for 1h and then irradiated under sunlight for 30min. After irradiation, the brown precipitates were collected by centrifugation and repeatedly washed with deionized water and dried at 60°C overnight. The ternary composites were obtained by adding appropriate amounts of AgNO3 (0.5, 1, 2wt%) and defined as 0.5A2CCNNs, 1A2CCNNs, and 2A2CCNNs, respectively.

The crystal structure and phase purity of the prepared samples were analyzed by Xʼ pert pro powder X-ray diffraction (Netherlands) using the Cu-Kα radiation (λ =1.5406Å). Field-emission scanning electron microscopy with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy was performed on a Carl Zeiss ultra 55 microscope; EDS spectra and maps were recorded using oxford instruments X-MaxN SDD (50mm2) system and INCA analysis software to characterize the morphologies and compositions of the prepared samples. Furthermore, the morphologies and compositions of the products were also examined by transmission electron microscopy, which was recorded on a JEOL/JEM 2100 operating at 200kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy data were obtained on a Kratos AXIS Ultra spectrometer with Al Kα radiation at 1486.71eV. The photoluminescence spectra were measured on a Shimadzu RF-5301PC spectrofluorophotometer with an excitation wavelength of 360nm. Fourier transform infrared spectra were recorded on Shimadzu IR Prestige-21 spectrophotometer. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy was performed on a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrophotometer using BaSO4 as the reference. Time-resolved transient PL spectra were obtained from an HORIBA JOBIN – VYON lifetime spectrophotometer at an excitation wavelength of 370nm.

The photocatalytic activities of the as-prepared photocatalysts were evaluated by the degradation of MO and PNP under sunlight irradiation. The degradation experiments were carried out under similar conditions in the month of June 2016 at Osmania University, and the sunlight intensity was measured using an LT Lutron LX-101A digital light meter, and the average intensity was found to be around 100,000lux. In a typical photocatalytic experiment, 50mg of the photocatalyst was dispersed in 50mL aqueous solution of MO or PNP (10mgl−1). Prior to light irradiation, the suspensions were kept in the dark under stirring for 1h to ensure an adsorption–desorption equilibrium between photocatalyst and pollutants. Subsequently, the suspensions were irradiated with direct sunlight. At a given irradiation time intervals (20min), 3mL of the suspension was withdrawn and centrifuged to separate the catalyst from the aqueous solution, then the residual dye concentration was analyzed by UV–vis spectrophotometer. A blank test was also carried out without any photocatalyst under sunlight irradiation, to evaluate the efficiency of the photocatalyst. Further, the cycling tests were also carried out to measure the stability of catalysts under sunlight irradiation.

To investigate the active species responsible for photodegradation, a series of radicals trapping experiments were performed. For this, an appropriate scavenger was added to the catalyst-dye suspension and the degradation experimental processes were similar to that of photocatalysis. In this study, iso-propanol (IPA, 1mM), ammonium oxalate (AO, 1mM), and 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ, 1mM) were used as the hydroxyl (·OH), hole (h+) and superoxide radical (·O2 −) scavenger, respectively [41,42].

The synthetic strategy of the ACCNNs is shown in Scheme 1 . First, we synthesized CNNs from the BCN through a liquid exfoliation method and CDs by simple autoclave and then developed CCNNs composites using an impregnation-hydrothermal method. In this synthetic process, CDs can be adsorbed effectively onto CNNs due to their similar graphite-like structures and the strong interaction between carboxyl groups of CDs and amino groups of CNNs. These oxygenous groups and amino groups also played an important role in adsorbing and stabilizing Ag+ ions to form a homogeneous suspension of Ag+/CCNNs upon the addition of AgNO3. During the photochemical synthetic process, CDs acted as a photosensitizer because of their broad visible light absorption under sunlight and injected the electron to CNNs and simultaneously reduce the surrounding Ag+ to Ag0 [43,44], finally resulting in the formation of AgNPs deposited CCNNs composites.

XRD patterns of BCN, CNNs and a series of CCNNs and ACCNNs composite samples are shown in Fig. 1 . From Fig. 1a, BCN shows a strong peak at 27.4° (002) and weak peak 13.1° (100) corresponding to the inter-layer stacking of aromatic systems and in-plane structural packing motif of tri-s-triazine units of g-C3N4, respectively [45,46]. Compared with BCN, the diffraction peaks of CNNs significantly decreased, which demonstrated that the BCN has been successfully exfoliated into nanosheets [38]. Besides, the (002) diffraction peak position for CNNs slightly shifted to the higher angle (Fig. 1b), indicating a reduction in the stacking distance of the layer structure after the formation of nanosheets which is in accordance with previous reports [47]. However, in the case of composites, similar patterns were observed, implying that the structure of CNNs has not been affected by the introduction of CDs and AgNPs. And no diffraction peak of CDs and AgNPs were observed, which may be due to its limited amount in composites. In addition, the dominant peak becomes weaker gradually with the increase in CDs and AgNPs contents, suggesting that CDs and AgNPs have been successfully deposited onto the surface of CNNs. Similar results were observed in previous g-C3N4 based composites [45].

FTIR spectra of BCN, CNNs and a series of CCNNs and ACCNNs composite samples are shown in Fig. 2 . In the FTIR spectrum of BCN, the broad peak at 3000–3500cm−1 is attributed to stretching vibration of NH groups and surface-adsorbed hydroxyl groups [45,48,49]. Several peaks located in the range of 1200–1650cm−1 attributed to the stretching vibration of CN heterocycles, while the sharp peak at 808cm−1 can be attributed to the characteristic breathing mode of s-triazine units [48–50]. Compared with BCN, CNNs and its composites had similar absorption peaks, which indicated that the exfoliation process and the introduction of CDs, AgNPs did not destroy the g-C3N4 basic structure. This is in consistent with the XRD results and previous reports [51]. Furthermore, the peaks of composites were slightly shifted to lower wavenumber, suggesting that there might be some interactions between the CDs, AgNPs and CNNs.

XPS was used to investigate the surface composition and chemical status of the as-prepared photocatalysts and shown in Fig. 3 . Fig. 3(a) shows the survey spectrum of CNNs which contains C, N and O elements, and the survey spectrum of 1A2CCNNs sample which contains C, N, O and Ag elements. The XPS peak of O 1s in CNNs might be due to the tiny amount of O2 adsorbed on the surface of the synthetic product during the polymerization process, which is a common phenomenon in the synthetic g-C3N4 materials [39]. As observed in Fig. 3b and c, the high-resolution C 1s spectra of both CNNs and 1A2CCNNs has two obvious peaks at 284.6 and 288.1eV, corresponding to sp2 CC bonds and sp2 bonded carbon (NCN) in the s-triazine rings, which are the major carbon species in the g-C3N4 [52]. Interestingly, a new peak at 285.6eV (COH) was obviously observed in 1A2CCNNs composite, which might be caused by the significant interaction between g-C3N4 and CDs [53,54]. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum of both CNNs and 1A2CCNNs (Fig. 3d and e) can be resolved into three characteristic peaks at 398.8, 399.8, and 401.1eV, which are ascribed to sp2-hybridized (CNC), tertiary (N(C)3) groups and amino functional groups (CNH) [52], respectively. Notably, the appearance of a new peak (COH), and the increase of CC peak as well as (N(C)3) and (CNH) peaks of the composite compared to those of CNNs illustrates the deposition of CDs [53,54]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of the Ag 3d of the 1A2CCNNs, as shown in Fig. 3f, demonstrates two peaks at 368.2 and 374.2eV with a spin energy separation of 6.0eV, corresponding to the binding energies of Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2, indicating that silver in the 1A2CCNNs is of metallic nature [55,56]. From the XPS results, we can expect the strong interfacial coupling effect between CDs, AgNPs and CNNs, which could promote photogenerated electron–hole pairs separation and transfer, and further enhance the photocatalytic performance of AgNPs/CDs/CNNs composites.

The morphology and microstructure of the as-prepared samples were characterized by FE-SEM and HR-TEM analysis. The FE-SEM image of BCN (Fig. 4a) displayed a layered structure composed of tightly stacked g-C3N4 nanosheets with several micrometers in size, whereas the CNNs (Fig. 4b) consisted of thin sheet-like structures with wrinkles and irregular shapes, which further confirmed the successful exfoliation of BCN. In the case of 1A2CCNNs (Fig. 4c), it can be clearly seen that the morphology of CNNs remained intact, indicating that no significant change occurred during the preparation of composites and a large number of small dots were uniformly distributed on CNNs surface, which might be a strong evidence for successful deposition of CDs and AgNPs. The TEM image of CNNs (Fig. 4d) exhibited thin, smooth and flat layer nanosheet structure, which may result in the increased specific surface area and reactive sites [47]. From the TEM image of 1A2CCNNs (Fig. 4e), it is can be clearly observed that two different types of particles are present on the CNNs. The bigger particles, which are appearing like dark spots and having size distribution (10–20nm) may be designated as silver nanoparticles and the small particles with low contrast and sizes around (5–10nm) can be ascribed as carbon dots. It is clear from the previous reports that the carbon dots are amorphous in nature with a low degree of crystallization and they will appear as low contrast spots in TEM [57,58]. HRTEM results provided further confirmation. In the HRTEM image shown in Fig. 4f, particles with different lattice fringe spacings (0.32 and 0.24) were observed, which further confirms the co-existence of two types of nanoparticles on CNNs surface. The lattice fringe spacings of 0.32nm (002) and 0.24nm (111) corresponding to the crystal plane of CDs [51] and metallic Ag [55,59], respectively. These results strongly prove that both CDs and AgNPs are successfully deposited on the surface of CNNs.

To further confirm the coexistence of CDs and AgNPs, elemental mapping was done (Fig. 5 ), which clearly shows that C, N, Ag, and O elements were uniformly distributed over the 1A2CCNNs materials. In addition to this, EDS analysis was employed to elucidate the chemical composition of the 1A2CCNNs and the results are shown in Fig. 5e, which also reveals the presence of only C, N, Ag, and O elements indicating that the material was free from impurities.

The optical absorbance spectra of BCN, CNNs and a series of CCNNs and ACCNNs composite samples are shown in Fig. 6 . As shown in Fig. 6a, the BCN has an absorption edge at 460nm, corresponding to the optical band gap of 2.7eV [53], while the band edge of CNNs displays a slight blue shift compared with BCN, which is attributed to the quantum confinement effect of g-C3N4 nanosheets and in consistent with previous reports [39]. In the case of composites, no shift in the band edge was observed, but the intensity in the visible region increased with increase in the amount of CDs, which can be ascribed to the intrinsic optical absorbance of CDs [53]. Furthermore, after deposition of AgNPs on the surface of 2CCNNs, a new absorption peak appears in the visible region (450–580nm) (Fig. 6b), and the absorbance of A2CCNNs increases gradually with AgNPs loading. The new broad peak in the visible region is attributed to the SPR of AgNPs [60]. These properties are favorable for better utilization of the visible region of the solar spectrum and in turn, improve the photocatalytic performance.

PL emission spectra of as-prepared samples that excited at 360nm were recorded in order to further assess the migration, transfer, and recombination process of the photogenerated electron–hole pairs. The PL emission is obtained as a result of recombination of the excited electron–hole pair, hence the lower PL intensity indicates efficient charge carrier separation [61]. Fig. 7a shows the PL spectra of BCN, CNNs and a series of CCNNs and ACCNNs composite samples. As shown in a Fig. 7a, the emission peak of CNNs shows a slight blue shift in comparison with BCN which can be ascribed to the quantum confinement effect of g-C3N4 nanosheets [39]. The decrease in the PL intensity of CNNs was observed after forming composites with CDs, indicating that the loading of CDs played a role in suppressing the recombination of the photogenerated carriers [51]. Moreover, the intensity of the peak further decreased after introducing the AgNPs into the CCNNs sample, indicating the superior separation of electron–hole pairs, and as a result, the photocatalytic activity is significantly enhanced.

The time-resolved transient PL spectra were further employed to examine the charge transfer dynamics of the samples (Fig. 7b). The curves can be fitted well using a bi-exponential decay function and the fitted lifetimes are listed in Table 1 . The short lifetime (τ 1) and long lifetimes (τ 2) are 2.21 and 9.81ns for 2CCNNs, 1.98 and 8.02ns for 1A2CCNNs, respectively, which are significantly lower than the lifetimes of CNNs (2.36 and τ 2 =10.43ns), which demonstrates that some excited electrons undergo rapid transfer from CNNs to CDs and AgNPs, thereby reducing the recombination of photogenerated electron–hole pairs. The similar phenomenon was also observed in the previous investigations. Can Xue et al. prepared In2O3-g-C3N4 hybrids, and it found that the lifetime is shortened after coupling g-C3N4 with In2O3 [62]. The shortened lifetimes originated from the formation of In2O3-g-C3N4 hybrids, and it effectively reduced the recombination of photogenerated electron–hole pairs. Therefore, formation of the heterojunction between the CDs, AgNPs and CNNs remarkably promotes charge carrier transfer efficiency, and thereby favors the photocatalytic reactions.

The photocatalytic activities of as-prepared samples were evaluated by the degradation of MO and PNP under sunlight irradiation and shown in Fig. 8 . Before the light irradiation, adsorption process between pollutants and the samples were almost reached to equilibrium at the time of 30min. It is noted that the adsorption capacity of photocatalysts increases with increasing of CDs content, which can be ascribed to the enhanced π–π interaction between the conjugated structure of CDs and the benzene ring of pollutants [33]. The superior adsorption capacity is in favor of improving photocatalytic activity because of the full contact between target molecules and photocatalysts. As shown in Fig. 8a and b, no obvious degradation of MO and PNP was observed in the absence of any catalyst, indicating that the photolysis can be ignored and the degradation behavior was driven primarily by a photocatalytic process. Under sunlight irradiation, only 12 and 33% of MO was degraded by BCN and CNNs, respectively within 100min. The improved activity of CNNs is mainly due to its increased specific surface area and more reactive sites resulted from the formation of nanosheets [38]. After deposition of CDs, the CNNs degradation efficiency was significantly increased (Fig. 8a), when the mass ratio of CDs is 2wt%, maximum efficiency (86%) was observed and further increasing the amount of CDs resulted in the decreased photocatalytic activity, which may be due to that too many CDs on CNNs would limit the light absorption by covering its surface and can also act as a recombination site for charge carriers [51]. Moreover, the photocatalytic activity of the A2CCNNs containing different amount of AgNPs is further investigated. When the amount of AgNPs increase from 0.5 to 1wt%, the photocatalytic activity of A2CCNNs is reached to maximum and degradation rate of MO reaches to 98.6% in 100min. The rate of degradation was decreased with an increase in the amount of AgNPs from 1 to 2wt%. Here the excess amount of AgNPs lead to a decrease in the degradation efficiency, which may be because of the fact, that the overloading of AgNPs can result in a shielding effect that weakens the light harvesting capacity of A2CCNNs and act as a recombination center [55].

Photocatalytic degradation of PNP under sunlight irradiation was also carried out. As shown in Fig. 8b, 37, 87, and 92% of PNP was degraded over CNNs, 2CCNNs, and 1A2CCNNs for 100min. The result reveals again 1A2CCNNs composite was exhibited high photocatalytic performance under sunlight irradiation. The characteristic absorbance variations of MO and PNP over 1A2CCNNs shown in Figs. S1a and S1b. It is clearly seen that the intensities of MO and PNP decrease gradually with respect to reaction time, revealing the degradation of pollutants.

Photocatalytic degradation of PNP under sunlight irradiation was also carried out. As shown in Fig. 8b, 37, 87, and 92% of PNP was degraded over CNNs, 2CCNNs, and 1A2CCNNs for 100min. The result reveals again 1A2CCNNs composite was exhibited high photocatalytic performance under sunlight irradiation. The characteristic absorbance variations of MO and PNP over 1A2CCNNs shown in Figs. S1a and S1b. It is clearly seen that the intensities of MO and PNP decrease gradually with respect to reaction time, revealing the degradation of pollutants.

The kinetics of MO and PNP photocatalytic degradation in the presence of as-prepared samples were fitted well with the pseudo-first-order kinetics model and shown in Fig. 9 . The degradation rate constants of MO (Fig. 9a) over BCN, CNNs, 1CCNNs, 2CCNNs, 4CCNNs, 0.5A2CCNNs, 1A2CCNNs, and 2A2CCNNs photocatalysts under sunlight irradiation were calculated to be 0.00121min−1, 0.00370min−1, 0.0159min−1, 0.0201min−1, 0.0182min−1, 0.0263min−1, 0.0426min−1, and 0.0293min−1, respectively. And for PNP (Fig. 9b), the rate constants of the CNNs, 2CCNNs, and 1A2CCNNs photocatalysts are 0.00444min−1, 0.0206min−1, and 0.0259min−1, respectively. The rate constant of 1A2CCNNs for MO degradation is about 35.2, 11.5 and 2.1 times higher than that of the BCN, CNNs, and 2CCNNs and which was for PNP degradation is also about 5.8 and 1.2 times higher than that of the CNNs and 2CCNNs.

The photocatalytic performance of CNNs and 1A2CCNNs was further investigated under NIR light irradiation (9W NIR light with the wavelength of 850nm) (Fig. S2). As shown in Fig. S2, 1A2CCNNs composite exhibited significant photocatalytic activity (22%), whereas pure CNNs showed negligible activity. This enhanced photocatalytic activity might be ascribed to the upconversion property of CDs, by which CDs absorb NIR radiation and emit Visible light which in turn excite g-C3N4 and generates charge carriers [54]. To determine whether the photodegradation is induced by NIR light or by the photothermal effect, we carried out control experiment at 50°C in the dark, according to the achieved temperature of catalyst solution during NIR light irradiation for 100min. The obtained results (Fig. S2) showed that no obvious degradation was observed after 100min of heating, indicating that the catalytic activity of 1A2CCNNs was mainly caused from the photocatalysis reaction, not NIR light produced temperature effect.

The photocatalytic performance of CNNs and 1A2CCNNs was further investigated under NIR light irradiation (9W NIR light with the wavelength of 850nm) (Fig. S2). As shown in Fig. S2, 1A2CCNNs composite exhibited significant photocatalytic activity (22%), whereas pure CNNs showed negligible activity. This enhanced photocatalytic activity might be ascribed to the upconversion property of CDs, by which CDs absorb NIR radiation and emit Visible light which in turn excite g-C3N4 and generates charge carriers [54]. To determine whether the photodegradation is induced by NIR light or by the photothermal effect, we carried out control experiment at 50°C in the dark, according to the achieved temperature of catalyst solution during NIR light irradiation for 100min. The obtained results (Fig. S2) showed that no obvious degradation was observed after 100min of heating, indicating that the catalytic activity of 1A2CCNNs was mainly caused from the photocatalysis reaction, not NIR light produced temperature effect.

All the above results clearly demonstrate that the constructed ACCNNs ternary composites have more intense photocatalytic activities for degrading organic pollutants than that of CCNNs, CNNs, and BCN, respectively. It implies that the synergistic effect existed between AgNPs, CDs, and CNNs, which can promote the charge carrier separation and then markedly increase the photoactivity.

The stability and reusability of the photocatalyst are vital in practical applications. For investigating the long-term stability of as-prepared composite (1A2CCNNs) recycling reactions were performed by degrading MO under sunlight irradiation. After every 100min of photodegradation, the catalyst was separated and washed with deionized water and oven dried for the next run. As shown in Fig. 10 , only a slight decrease in the photocatalytic activity was observed up to four cycles of degradation. Hence, the as-prepared composites can be used as effective photocatalysts for pollutants degradation with good stability. Furthermore, XRD and FTIR patterns of the 1A2CCNNs composite before and after (4th run) the photocatalytic reactions are shown in Figs. S3a and S3b, which clearly suggest that the catalyst is stable during the reactions.

The stability and reusability of the photocatalyst are vital in practical applications. For investigating the long-term stability of as-prepared composite (1A2CCNNs) recycling reactions were performed by degrading MO under sunlight irradiation. After every 100min of photodegradation, the catalyst was separated and washed with deionized water and oven dried for the next run. As shown in Fig. 10 , only a slight decrease in the photocatalytic activity was observed up to four cycles of degradation. Hence, the as-prepared composites can be used as effective photocatalysts for pollutants degradation with good stability. Furthermore, XRD and FTIR patterns of the 1A2CCNNs composite before and after (4th run) the photocatalytic reactions are shown in Figs. S3a and S3b, which clearly suggest that the catalyst is stable during the reactions.

To investigate the photocatalytic degradation mechanism of pollutants over these composites, the effect of different scavengers on the degradation rate was examined and the results are shown in Fig. 11 . It is clear from Fig. 11, that the addition of IPA did not effect the degradation rate of MO, suggesting that OH radical were not the main reactive species in the photocatalytic process. In contrast, as compared with blank (without scavenger), the degradation of MO was decreased by 90 and 37% in the presence of BQ and AO respectively, suggesting that the O2 − and h+ are the main reactive species.

Moreover, the PL spectra was also performed for identification of OH generation during photocatalytic reactions, by using terephthalic acid (TA) as a probe molecule [61], which readily reacts with OH to produce highly fluorescent 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid having a λ max at ∼425nm. The weak PL signal located at about 425nm (Fig. S4) demonstrated that minor OH was formed, which was consistent with the IPA quenching results.

Moreover, the PL spectra was also performed for identification of OH generation during photocatalytic reactions, by using terephthalic acid (TA) as a probe molecule [61], which readily reacts with OH to produce highly fluorescent 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid having a λ max at ∼425nm. The weak PL signal located at about 425nm (Fig. S4) demonstrated that minor OH was formed, which was consistent with the IPA quenching results.

Based on the above experimental results we proposed a tentative mechanism for degradation of organic pollutants over 1A2CCNNs composite as shown Fig. 12 . Under sunlight irradiation, the electrons were promoted from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB) of g-C3N4, leaving the hole behind. At this time, CDs act as electron acceptors to trap a portion of electrons excited from CNNs, thus hindering the recombination of electron–hole pairs and they can also act as a photosensitizer to sensitize CNNs and donate the electrons to the CB of CNNs [51,63], resulting in the extended visible light response region for photocatalytic degradation. Meanwhile, the excited electrons on CB of CNNs and the injected electrons from CDs transport to AgNPs through the formed Schottky barrier, thus facilitating the charge separation in the whole photocatalytic system. This is in consistent with the PL results. Then these electrons react with oxygen molecules absorbed on the surface of the catalyst to produce the O2 −. At the same time, CDs can absorb longer wavelength light, and emit shorter wavelength light as a result of their upconversion property [53,54,63] and the SPR effect of AgNPs can induce enhancement of the local electric field [60], these processes subsequently excite CNNs to produce more charge carriers giving a positive influence on the photocatalytic activity of the 1A2CCNNs composite. The photogenerated hole on the VB of CNNs cannot react with OH− or H2O molecules to five OH radicals, because of the VB potential of the g-C3N4 (+1.57eV) is more negative than that of OH/OH− (+1.99eV) [55,60]. Instead, the holes directly oxidize the adsorbed pollutants. The O2 − and h+ are responsible for the degradation of pollutants.

In summary, we have successfully prepared new ternary composites by depositing CDs and AgNPs on CNNs surface to degrade the organic pollutants. First, we have prepared CCNNs composites with different wt% of CDs. Then the resulting composite which showed enhanced photocatalytic activity among them (2 CCNNs) was used for making ternary composites. 1A2CCNNs exhibited the best photocatalytic activity for degradation of organic pollutants. Different characterization techniques revealed that an intimate contact exists between the AgNPs, CDs, and CNNs. Due to the coexistence of CDs and AgNPs on CNNs, separation of photogenerated electron–hole pairs and utilization of solar spectrum were distinctly improved, which in turn enhanced the photocatalytic performance. In addition, 1A2CCNNs composite displayed excellent stability and reusability, which is valuable for the potential applications of environmental protection. Photogenerated holes and superoxide radicals were found to be the main active species responsible for the photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants. As a whole, this work can provide important inspirations in developing other CDs and AgNPs based ternary composites for enlarging the family of photocatalysts.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: One of the authors (Ramakrishna Dadigala) gratefully acknowledges UGC-INDIA for Senior Research Fellowship. The authors would like to thank STIC India for extending their Transmission Electron Microscope facility. The authors also grateful to SAIF, the DST unit on nanoscience, IIT Madras for providing analytical facilities. The authors also thankful to Materials Research Centre, MNIT, Jaipur, India for providing XPS facility. Sincere thanks to DST-FIST, New Delhi, India. Department of Chemistry, Osmania University (OU) and CFRD-OU for providing infrastructure and other necessary facilities.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2017.03.032.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:
